content.claim: Some elements of Poland's 1999 reform proved politically irreversible: increased school autonomy, external examinations, broader access to preschool, and higher-education access changes remained in place even after the broader 2016–2019 reversal, suggesting that decentralized and legally-entrenched reforms resist rollback better than central mandates alone.
content.context: Early selection at age 10–12 into the 6- and 8-year grammar school tracks is justifiable only if Hungary can sustain politically durable institutional design, ensure between-school equity gains translate into individual-level SES equalization, and maintain implementation continuity across electoral cycles—outcomes Poland achieved partially and lost entirely, demonstrating that the benefits of delayed selection (1 SD gain for at-risk students, improved labor-market outcomes, reduced school segregation) outweigh the benefits of early tracking only if political reversibility risk and individual-SES gains (not achieved in Poland) are weighted equally with test-score gains.
limitations: The v1 source string was not re-verified during migration.
content.population: Population described in the migrated claim
content.time_scope: As reported in the v1 source corpus
